---
title: "VS Code and Copilot"
---

# VS Code and Copilot

An IDE-based alternative path. Copilot in VS Code is a different shape of tool from a CLI agent — useful to know both.

## Overview

This guide helps you set up:

1. Visual Studio Code
2. **Either** Python **or** R in VS Code
3. GitHub Copilot in VS Code

Estimated time: 20-40 minutes.

::: {.callout-note}
## Terminal commands in this guide
This setup uses several terminal commands (for checks like `code --version`, `python --version`, and `R --version`).
If you are new to terminals, read [Terminal Basics](terminal-basics.html) first.
:::

---

## Before You Start

- A stable internet connection
- A GitHub account (create one at <a href="https://github.com/" target="_blank">github.com</a> if needed)
- Permission to install software on your computer

---

## Step 1: Choose Your OS

Select your OS once, then stay in that tab for the language setup so you only see one platform path.

Official docs:

 - <a href="https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/setup/windows" target="_blank">VS Code on Windows</a>
 - <a href="https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/setup/mac" target="_blank">VS Code on macOS</a>

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Windows

#### Install VS Code

1. Download VS Code from <a href="https://code.visualstudio.com/Download" target="_blank">code.visualstudio.com</a>
2. Run `VSCodeUserSetup-{version}.exe`
3. Keep the default **User setup** install unless you specifically need system-wide install
4. Reopen terminal after install

Quick check:

```bash
code --version
```

#### Step 2: Choose One Language Path

Pick one language to start. You can install the other one later.

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Python

Official docs:

 - <a href="https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/python" target="_blank">Python in VS Code</a>
 - <a href="https://www.python.org/downloads/windows/" target="_blank">Python downloads (Windows)</a>

##### Install Python + VS Code support

1. Install the latest stable Python 3 from <a href="https://www.python.org/downloads/windows/" target="_blank">python.org</a>
2. Open a new terminal and check:

```bash
py --version
```

If `py` is not available, try:

```bash
python --version
```

3. Open VS Code
4. Open Extensions (`Ctrl+Shift+X`)
5. Install **Python** (publisher: Microsoft)
6. Run Command Palette and select: `Python: Select Interpreter`
7. Choose your installed Python interpreter

##### Test Python

Create `hello.py`:

```python
print("Hello from Python in VS Code")
```

Run it with the play button ("Run Python File") or terminal.

#### R

Official docs:

 - <a href="https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/r" target="_blank">R in VS Code</a>
 - <a href="https://cloud.r-project.org/" target="_blank">Install R</a>

##### Install R + VS Code support

1. Install R from <a href="https://cloud.r-project.org/" target="_blank">cloud.r-project.org</a>
2. During install, keep defaults (recommended: let installer store version info in registry)
3. Open a new terminal and check:

```bash
R --version
```

4. In VS Code Extensions, install **R** (publisher: REditorSupport)
5. Install `languageserver` in R:

```r
install.packages("languageserver")
```

This package enables IntelliSense-style features in VS Code (autocomplete, diagnostics, and hover help).

6. In VS Code, open Command Palette and run: `R: Create R terminal`

##### Test R

Create `hello.R`:

```r
x <- 2 + 2
print(x)
```

Run with `Ctrl+Enter` in an R terminal.

:::

### macOS

#### Install VS Code

1. Download VS Code from [code.visualstudio.com](https://code.visualstudio.com/Download)
2. Open the `.dmg`
3. Drag **Visual Studio Code.app** into **Applications**
4. Open VS Code, and open Command Palette (`Cmd+Shift+P`) to run:
   - `Shell Command: Install 'code' command in PATH`
5. Open a terminal in VS Code

Quick check:

```bash
code --version
```

#### Step 2: Choose One Language Path

Pick one language to start. You can install the other one later.

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Python

Official docs:

 - <a href="https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/python" target="_blank">Python in VS Code</a>
 - <a href="https://www.python.org/downloads/macos/" target="_blank">Python downloads (macOS)</a>

##### Install Python + VS Code support

1. Install the latest stable Python 3 from <a href="https://www.python.org/downloads/macos/" target="_blank">python.org</a>
2. Open a new terminal and check:

```bash
python3 --version
```

::: {.callout-note}
If you are on macOS, you may be prompted to install Xcode Command Line Tools when running Python or opening a terminal for the first time. Follow the prompts to install if needed.
:::

3. Open VS Code
4. Open Extensions (`Cmd+Shift+X`)
5. Install **Python** (publisher: Microsoft)
6. Run Command Palette and select: `Python: Select Interpreter`
7. Choose your installed Python interpreter

##### Test Python

Create `hello.py`:

```python
print("Hello from Python in VS Code")
```

Run it with the play button ("Run Python File") or terminal.

#### R

Official docs:

 - <a href="https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/r" target="_blank">R in VS Code</a>
 - <a href="https://cloud.r-project.org/" target="_blank">Install R</a>

##### Install R + VS Code support

1. Install R from <a href="https://cloud.r-project.org/" target="_blank">cloud.r-project.org</a>
2. Open a new terminal and check:

```bash
R --version
```

3. In VS Code Extensions, install **R** (publisher: REditorSupport)
4. Install `languageserver` in R:

```r
install.packages("languageserver")
```

This package enables IntelliSense-style features in VS Code (autocomplete, diagnostics, and hover help).

5. In VS Code, open Command Palette and run: `R: Create R terminal`

##### Test R

Create `hello.R`:

```r
x <- 2 + 2
print(x)
```

Run with `Cmd+Enter` in an R terminal.

:::

:::

---

## Step 2: Set Up GitHub Copilot in VS Code

Official docs:

 - <a href="https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/setup" target="_blank">Set up GitHub Copilot in VS Code</a>
 - <a href="https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/get-started/plans" target="_blank">GitHub Copilot plans</a>

1. Open VS Code
2. In the status bar, click the Copilot icon and choose **Use AI Features**
3. Sign in to GitHub when prompted
4. If you do not already have a plan, you can start with **Copilot Free**
5. Open a code file and verify Copilot is active (chat panel opens and/or inline suggestions appear)

### Student note

If you are a verified student, apply for the free student Copilot Pro plan ("student plus" upgrade).

Quick quota diff (checked February 17, 2026):

- **Copilot Free:** 2,000 code completions/month, 50 chat messages/month, and 50 premium requests/month
- **Student plan (Copilot Pro at $0):** unlimited code completions, unlimited chat interactions in IDE/CLI/mobile, and 300 premium requests/month

If you are eligible, this is a large jump in included usage, so it is worth applying early.

 - <a href="https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/how-tos/manage-your-account/get-free-access-to-copilot-pro" target="_blank">Get free access to Copilot Pro (students/teachers/maintainers)</a>

---

## Quick Troubleshooting

### `code` command not found

- Windows: close and reopen terminal after install
- macOS: run `Shell Command: Install 'code' command in PATH` from Command Palette, then restart terminal

### Python interpreter not found in VS Code

1. Run `Python: Select Interpreter`
2. If nothing appears, reinstall Python and restart VS Code

### R not found (Windows)

You might need to add the R.exe location such as 'C:\Program Files\R\R-4.3.3\bin' to 'PATH'

```
Start → search “Environment Variables” → “Edit the system environment variables”, 
Environment Variables…
Under your user (or System) variables: Path → Edit → New
Paste the ...\bin path → OK → reopen cmd
```

### R extension works but no IntelliSense

Install in R:

```r
install.packages("languageserver")
```

Then restart VS Code.

### Copilot sign-in issues

1. In VS Code, use Accounts menu to sign out/in GitHub
2. Check your Copilot plan at <a href="https://github.com/settings/copilot" target="_blank">github.com/settings/copilot</a>
